title: practical english usage - Part 45
content:
For the simple past with a present or future meaning (e.g. It's time you went), ►    46.

One day, the Princess decided...  I saw Jack yesterday.

simple     past    with    finished      time    expressions we use it if we  do not have a special reason for using one of the other tenses.

In general, the simple past tense is the normal one for talking about the past; I saw Jack yesterday morning. He told me ...

The  simple past is often used with words referring to finished times.

so she told her father that she wanted to get a Job ...

One day the Princess decided that she didn't like staying at home all day, The  simple past is common in stories and descriptions of past events.

Regularly every summer,  Jessica fell in love.

I spent all my childhood in Scotland.

Daniel broke a window last night.

actions and happenings, longer situations, and repeated events.

We   use the simple past for many kinds of past events: short, quickly finished 4 use vowel is not stressed: 'travel -> travelled.

In British English, -I is doubled in the past after one short vowel even if the Verbs ending in -c have ck in the past (e.g. picnic -» picnicked).

But (vowel + -y):                                   play -> played study studied change y to i and add -ed     cry -> cried Verbs ending in consonant + -y:          hurry -> hurried 'visit -> visited But (last syllable not stressed):    'θjgfer  offered regret -> regretted double the consonant and add -ed refer -> referred + one consonant (except w or y):        plan -» planned Verbs ending in one stressed vowel    shop -> shopped Section5 Past   and   Perfect     Tenses grammar •  45  past progressive 1 saw James.

refers to a shorter action or event that happened in the middle of the longer progressive refers to a longer 'background' action or situation; the simple past We   often use the past progressive together with a simple past tense.
